data.title: China’s share of US imports drops to 7.1%, lowest since 2001
data.description:
• China's share of US imports fell to 7.1% in May, the lowest level since 2001, according to trade data published by the US Census Bureau on Thursday.

  • That’s 4.3 percentage points lower than the same period last year and reflects a longer-term decline that began during President Donald Trump’s first term.

  • China’s share of imports was as high as 14.8% in September 2024, before Trump returned to office and implemented steep new tariffs on imports from the country. 

  • Meanwhile, Taiwan and Vietnam each accounted for nearly 6% of US goods imports in May, up from roughly 3% in 2023.

data.title: Trump poised to set tariffs early, warns trade partners ahead of July 9 deadline
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said his administration could start sending letters to trade partners as early as Friday, setting unilateral tariff rates ahead of the July 9 deadline for negotiations.

“We’re probably going to be sending some letters out, starting probably tomorrow, maybe 10 a day to various countries saying what they’re going to pay to do business with the US,” Trump told reporters on Thursday as he left Washington for an event in Iowa.

He has repeatedly warned that if countries don't reach agreements with the U.S. before next week's deadline, he will unilaterally impose tariff rates, escalating pressure on trading partners scrambling to finalize deals with his administration.

So far, the Trump administration has reached trade deals with the UK and Vietnam and agreed to a truce with  China that saw the world’s two largest economies ease tit-for-tat tariffs.

ETFs to track the S&P (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: U.S. clears GE Aerospace to restart jet engine shipments to China - Reuters
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Commerce told GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) that it can restart jet engine shipments to China's state-owned aircraft manufacturer COMAC, Reuters reported late Thursday.

The license suspensions lifted for GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) affect LEAP-1C engines to COMAC for its C919 single-aisle aircraft, and GE's CF34 engine for COMAC's C909 regional jet, according to the report.

The C919 is made in China but many of its components come from overseas, and COMAC depends on GE Aerospace-made (GE) engines for its C919 aircraft - a jet model that seeks to rival Boeing and Airbus in the single-aisle market.

The U.S. action, combined with other recent moves such as the lifting of restrictions on exports to China for chip design software developers and ethane producers, marks a further sign of easing trade tensions between the two countries.

The status of licenses for Honeywell (HON) and RTX Corp.'s (RTX) Collins Aerospace, which also supply components for COMAC, is not clear.

data.title: U.S. oil and gas activity slumped in Q2, Dallas Fed says, pointing to higher steel tariffs
data.description:
U.S. oil and gas activity contracted in Q2, the latest energy survey from the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas said this week, revealing "slight pessimism" and "elevated uncertainty" among exploration and production companies and oilfield services firms.

The Dallas Fed's business activity index, a measure of market conditions, fell from positive 3.8 in Q1 to negative 8.1 in Q2, the index's lowest reading since 2020.

Many E&P firms are now saying they expect to drill fewer wells throughout 2025 than originally planned, with 26% of executives believing the number of wells they will drill this year will "decrease significantly." and 21% saying they will "decrease slightly."

Of the executives surveyed, 61% said their production would drop "slightly" if WTI crude remained at $60/bbl over the next year, but 46% said output would decrease "significantly" if WTI fell to $50/bbl.

In anonymous comments submitted along with the survey, many executives pointed specifically to President Trump's steel tariffs and his administration's repeated insistence on dropping prices as a hindrance to the market.

"The 'Liberation Day' chaos and tariff antics have harmed the domestic energy industry. 'Drill, baby, drill' will not happen with this level of volatility," one E&P company executive said in the survey.

Tariffs have raised tubular costs, and some companies in Texas and Oklahoma have seen a "sharp increase" in electricity costs, according to the comments.

"It's hard to imagine how much worse policies and D.C. rhetoric could have been for U.S. E&P companies," one executive said.

"The current political uncertainty is causing apprehension and concern about small, independent oil and gas companies' economic viability," another executive noted.

On Thursday, crude oil futures settled lower in quiet, range-bound trading ahead of the Fourth of July holiday weekend, ahead of the upcoming OPEC+ meeting which is expected to result in an agreement to raise output by 411,000 bbl/day in August.

In the U.S. earlier this week, a surprise build in crude inventories also highlighted demand concerns in the world's biggest crude consumer, and a private sector survey showed service activity in China, the world's biggest oil importer, expanded in June at its slowest pace in nine months as demand weakened.

On the positive side, U.S. June payrolls came in higher than expected and the unemployment rate fell, although resulting dollar gains could limit crude upside.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for August ended Thursday -0.7% to $67.00/bbl and front-month September Brent crude (CO1:COM) closed -0.4% to $68.80/bbl; since the end of last week, Nymex and Brent gained 2.2% and 1.5%, respectively.

U.S. natural gas futures fell going into the long weekend, with the front-month August contract -2.2% on Thursday and down 8.8% for the week to $3.409/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLE), finished +2%.

Source: Barchart.com

data.title: Petrobras outlines plans for $5.5B in Brazil refinery improvements
data.description:
Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) unveiled plans Thursday to invest ~30B reais ($5.5B) in refining and petrochemicals in Brazil's Rio de Janeiro state that align with the intent of President Lula da Silva's government to create jobs and accelerate economic growth.

Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) said an integration between the Reduc refinery at Duque de Caxias and the Boaventura energy complex would increase the production of S-10 diesel by 76K bbl/day and the production capacity of jet fuel by 20K bbl/day.

Brazil petrochem company Braskem (BAK) said it is also planning a 4.3B reais investment into a polyethylene plant near Reduc; Petrobras (PBR) owns 47% of Braskem's voting shares.

Petrobras (PBR) also is starting test production of fuels with renewable content, including "diesel R" that is made by processing fossil fuel with animal or vegetable oil, as well as sustainable aviation fuel.

"We're meeting the expectations not only of President Lula, but also of ourselves and society," CEO Magda Chambriard said at a press conference.

data.title: Gold futures fall as strong June jobs report slams rate cut hopes
data.description:
Gold futures finished with a gain for the week but ended down on Friday after the June jobs report showed more jobs added than expected by economists.

Stock market indexes and the U.S. dollar rose after non-farm payrolls increased by a larger than forecast 147,000 jobs last month, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics showed, as the domestic labor market showed surprising resilience and dampened market expectations for a significant easing of the Federal Reserve's monetary policy, creating a headwind for non-interest bearing bullion.

Prior to the release of the jobs data, the Fed was expected to cut interest rates by 25 basis points in September, with further cuts possible by the end of the year.

"The better than expected jobs number means we see a lesser likelihood of a Fed rate cut earlier than currently anticipated... The key is the fact that the idea or possibility of a July rate cut is off the table," High Ridge Futures director of metals trading David Meger said in a note.

Meanwhile, Republicans in the U.S. House of Representatives finally passed President Trump's massive tax and spending bill, which economists worry will add $3.3 trillion to the nation's debt.

Worries that President Trump's disruptive trade policies could slow global economic growth eased slightly this week after an agreement between the U.S. and Vietnam was announced ahead of a July 9 deadline when "reciprocal" U.S. tariffs are set to take effect.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) closed +1.7% to $3,331.60/oz and front-month July Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +2.1% to $36.784 this week; on Monday, gold fell 0.5% while silver added 1%.

data.title: U.S. drilling rigs decline for 10th straight week, Baker Hughes says
data.description:
The number of total oil and gas drilling rigs in the U.S. declined by 8 to 539, the lowest total since October 2021 and falling for the 10th week in a row for the first time since July 2020, Baker Hughes reported Friday in its latest survey.

The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by 7 to 425 this week, the lowest since September 2021, gas rigs shed 1 to 108, and 6 rigs remained classified as miscellaneous.

Baker Hughes said this week's decline dropped the total U.S. count by 46 rigs, or 8% less than the same time a year earlier.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: SA Asks: Which companies benefit from the U.S.-Vietnam trade deal?
data.description:
Which publicly traded companies are likely to benefit from the recently announced trade deal between the United States and Vietnam?

Seeking Alpha analysts Far Horizon and Grassroots weigh in.

Far Horizon: The Vietnam trade deal is definitely a positive for apparel retailers like Nike (NYSE:NKE), Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU) and Under Armour (UA). For example, half of Nike's shoes are made in Vietnam. They recently estimated that the tariff impact on costs would be $1 billion annually. As I explained in a recent article, the deal with Vietnam should save them over $300 million of this. 

As an economy projected to grow at over 5% annually for the rest of the decade, Vietnam's industrial expansion offers good opportunity for companies like Caterpillar (CAT), General Electric (GE) and Honeywell (HON) to trade with favorable terms. Vietnam's large population and agricultural segment are also attractive for agriculture and food companies like Archer Daniel Midland (ADM), John Deere (DE) and Tyson Food (TY).

Finally, investors might also look for opportunities to invest in Vietnam as a beneficiary of improved trade relations and U.S. investment. One simple way to do so is with sector ETFs like VanEcks Vietnam ETF (VNM).

Grassroots: July 2’s U.S.–Vietnam handshake takes Trump’s saber-rattling 46% “reciprocal” tariff and whittles it to a flat 20%, while U.S. exports, from Detroit metal to Midwest pork, walk into Vietnam toll-free. That’s instant oxygen for the brands that already shifted work south of the Chinese border. Nike (NYSE:NKE) and Under Armour (NYSE:UAA) make most of their shoes there, and Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) sources a mid-teens share of its denim line, so the deal removes a looming margin hit. 

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) breathes easier, too. Vietnam-built AirPods, iPads and Macs now face a survivable 20% duty instead of a crushing 46%, keeping Cupertino’s China-hedge alive. 

On the outbound ledger, Boeing (NYSE:BA) scores the marquee prize as Hanoi is poised to turn that long-floating memo into an $8 billion order for 50 737 MAX jets, a headline win for U.S. manufacturing.
